title: Focaccia, plain
subtitle: The Ligurian flatbread, oil-soaked and dimpled
excerpt: A high-hydration focaccia with a crisp underside, an open crumb, and enough olive oil to make the pan sizzle. The brine-and-oil solution poured over before baking is the Ligurian trick that keeps the top soft and seasoned.
sourceNotes: Liguria's focaccia Genovese is documented as a market bread from at least the sixteenth century; the oil-and-brine topping appears in Italian agricultural extension texts from the nineteenth century. The modern home version follows the method popularised by Samin Nosrat (Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat, 2017) but derives from the public-domain Ligurian pattern, not the modern book.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced once cool. Refresh in a 180°C fan oven for 6 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix and refrigerate overnight, covered. Remove from the fridge 90 minutes before baking and continue from the folding step.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, on the lower-middle shelf

glossaryTerms.definition: A dough where the water content is 75% or more of the flour weight by mass. Wet doughs develop an open, irregular crumb but are harder to handle by hand.

content.text: baked in an oiled tin at high heat. The tin bottom fries the base as the top bakes; the result is crisp below and open above, with a crust that comes away from the bread when you pick up a piece. This is not a project bread. The folding and resting does the structural work, so the total hands-on time is short despite the long overall schedule.

Whisk the yeast into the lukewarm water, then add the salt and olive oil. Pour over the flour and mix with a dough scraper or your hand until no dry flour remains. The dough will be very wet and loose. This is correct for 78% hydration. Cover the bowl and leave for 30 minutes. The gluten begins to develop during this rest without any kneading.

Every 30 minutes, perform a set of stretch and folds: wet your hand, reach under the dough, pull a portion up, and fold it back over the top. Rotate the bowl a quarter turn and repeat four times around the bowl. Do three sets total over 90 minutes. The dough will tighten and develop structure with each set.

Pour 40 ml of olive oil into a 30×20 cm tin and tilt to coat. Gently transfer the dough into the tin; do not deflate it. Press it out gently toward the corners. Cover and leave for 45 minutes; the dough will relax and spread to fill the tin.

Mix the brine: dissolve the salt in the water, then whisk in the olive oil until emulsified. Pour this over the dough and dimple it all over with your fingertips, pressing deeply to the bottom of the tin. The brine will pool in the dimples. Scatter with flaked salt.

Preheat the oven to 230°C fan. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes, until the surface is deep golden and the underside, when you lift a corner with a palette knife, is brown and crisp. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack. Eat the same day; the crust stays lively for about 4 hours.
